summary: Gruber analyzes rumors that Apple has already secured NFL Sunday Ticket rights, examining the strategic and financial implications of the deal. He considers whether Apple would bundle Sunday Ticket into the standard $4.99/month TV+ subscription or charge a premium, running back-of-the-envelope math showing Apple would need 33 million new subscribers to break even on a $2 billion annual deal. He notes the limitations of Sunday Ticket's appeal since it excludes local team games. Gruber draws a parallel to Fox's 1993 acquisition of NFC broadcast rights, which was less about direct profitability and more about establishing Fox as a major network. He concludes that Apple's goal should similarly be about cementing TV+ as a top-shelf streaming service rather than making money directly from the games.
key_points: Apple may have already secured NFL Sunday Ticket rights but is keeping the deal quiet until they choose to announce it, consistent with Apple's information control style
At $2 billion/year for the rights, Apple would need roughly 33 million new TV+ subscribers just to break even if included in the standard $4.99/month subscription
Sunday Ticket's appeal is inherently limited because it excludes local team games, which are what most NFL fans actually want to watch
If Apple charges a premium for Sunday Ticket access, Gruber expects they'll price it substantially below DirecTV's $300-400/season rates to make it a mass-market play

The Fox/NFC deal of 1993 is the right historical parallel — that deal was about establishing Fox as a legitimate network, and Sunday Ticket should be about establishing TV+ as a top-shelf streaming service
money_quotes: The question is, why does Apple want to stream NFL games? To make money directly from those games, or to get as many people as possible to try TV+ and eventually subscribe?
Half a billion dollars here, half a billion dollars there — it adds up.
"All the Sunday afternoon games except for your favorite team" is not a selling point for most NFL fans.
The point wasn't just to put the best NFL games on Fox, the point was to put Fox itself on the map, to establish Fox as a peer to the big three traditional networks.
To me, that should be the point of Apple securing the rights to Sunday Ticket. Not just to get NFL games on Apple TV+, but to further cement TV+ as a top-shelf streaming service.
The media industry is gossipy as hell, and word would likely leak from other parties, like Amazon and Disney.

I think there's a good chance that they'll charge substantially less than DirecTV's rates — that Apple will still try to make it more of a mass-market play for regular NFL fans, not just for superfans and gambling junkies.
key_insight: Apple's pursuit of NFL Sunday Ticket is best understood not as a content play to generate direct revenue, but as a strategic move — like Fox's 1993 NFC deal — to establish TV+ as a major streaming platform.
